title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text: This synthesis draws across the chapter notes for Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011).

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Program Theory
Program theory is the explicit account of how an intervention is expected to contribute to change. Across the book, it functions as a bridge between design, implementation, monitoring, evaluation, causal inference, and reporting. The recurring discipline is to make assumptions visible enough that they can be discussed, tested, revised, and used.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
section: Theory of Change and Theory of Action
text:
Theory of Change and Theory of Action
The book separates the theory of change from the theory of action. The theory of change explains the causal pathway from problem conditions to intended outcomes. The theory of action explains what the program does to activate that pathway: resources, activities, outputs, success criteria, implementation conditions, and assumptions about factors inside and outside the program’s control.
This distinction matters because a program may have a plausible desired change pathway but weak activities, or strong activities that do not address the causal factors identified in the situation analysis. The two theories need to be checked against each other.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Logic Models and Representation
Logic models are representations of program theory, not the theory itself. Pipeline models, outcomes chains, realist matrices, narratives, and systems-informed diagrams each foreground different features. The right representation depends on intended use, intervention complexity, and audience.
A good representation explains contribution, makes links meaningful, avoids dead ends, shows direction of change, and removes detail that does not help interpretation.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Assumptions and Context
Assumptions appear throughout the book as testable claims about how change happens, what context permits, and what factors affect success. Program theory is strongest when assumptions are explicit enough to guide evidence collection and humble enough to be revised.

title: Core Concepts in Purposeful Program Theory
text:
Causal Inference
Program theory supports causal inference by organizing congruence analysis, counterfactual comparison, and critical review. It does not replace causal evidence; it makes causal claims more inspectable by showing what patterns should be observed if the theory is operating.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Core Argument
Synthesis and reporting should use program theory to integrate diverse evidence, explain causal contribution, support timely learning, and connect findings across linked evaluations.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Study Summary
The final chapter focuses on how evidence is brought together and reported. For a single evaluation, program theory can organize diverse evidence along the outcomes chain, helping readers see how implementation, intermediate outcomes, final outcomes, context, and assumptions relate.
The authors also discuss rapid reporting and software-supported reporting. The key issue is not format but usefulness: reporting should make the evidence traceable to the theory and support decisions while the information is still actionable.
Across evaluations, program theory provides a structure for synthesis. It can connect findings from linked projects, compare evidence across different evaluations, and build cumulative understanding about what works, for whom, why, and under what conditions.

title: 16. Synthesis and Reporting
text:
Key Concepts

Theory-based synthesis: integrating evidence according to the causal structure of the program theory.
Rapid reporting: timely communication designed to support ongoing decisions.
Cross-evaluation synthesis: using program theory to compare and integrate findings across studies.
Traceability: making clear how evidence supports conclusions and judgements.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Core Argument
Program theory can anchor monitoring and evaluation plans by identifying what to measure, when to measure it, what comparisons are needed, and how evaluation questions connect to the expected causal pathway.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Study Summary
This chapter shows how program theory becomes operational in performance monitoring and evaluation design. It helps decide which aspects of the situation, outcomes, success criteria, assumptions, implementation features, and causal links should be measured.
The authors stress choice. A theory may contain many possible indicators, but a monitoring system must prioritize measures that are feasible, useful, and tied to decisions. Evaluation planning then uses the theory to identify timing, purposes, key questions, information needs, methods, and comparisons.
The chapter also considers complicated and complex programs. In such cases, evaluation may need to examine variation across strands, sites, contexts, or emergent pathways rather than assume one uniform model. Program theory is useful when it guides these design choices rather than becoming a static appendix.

title: 14. Developing a Monitoring and Evaluation Plan
text:
Key Concepts

Performance monitoring: routine tracking of selected aspects of implementation, context, outputs, outcomes, and assumptions.
Evaluation questions: focused questions that determine evidence needs and methods.
Comparison: a design feature used to interpret performance or causal contribution.
Theory-based measurement: choosing measures because they test or illuminate the program theory.

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Core Argument
Common program archetypes help evaluators recognize recurring intervention logics, but each archetype brings different assumptions, risks, and evidence needs.

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Study Summary
The chapter organizes recurring program forms such as advisory, information, education, incentives, deterrence, case management, community capacity building, and direct service delivery. These archetypes are useful because similar intervention forms often rely on similar causal assumptions.
Each archetype points to different change mechanisms. Information programs assume attention, comprehension, relevance, and ability to act. Incentives assume behavior responds to rewards. Deterrence assumes detection, sanction, and perceived risk. Case management assumes coordinated support can address multiple barriers. Capacity building assumes communities or organizations can acquire and use new capability.
The authors’ point is not to classify programs for its own sake. Archetypes help evaluators ask sharper questions about whether the chosen strategy fits the problem, what conditions are required, and what evidence would show that the mechanism is operating.

title: 12. Some Common Program Archetypes
text:
Key Concepts

Program archetype: a recurring intervention pattern with characteristic causal assumptions.
Carrots and sticks: incentive and deterrence approaches to changing behavior.
Case management: coordinated support addressing multiple participant needs or barriers.
Capacity building: strengthening ability to act at individual, organizational, or community levels.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Core Argument
A program theory should be critiqued for clarity, plausibility, evidence, context, ethics, usefulness, and proportionality before it is used as the basis for monitoring or evaluation.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Study Summary
The chapter provides criteria for reviewing program theory. Internal criteria examine whether the description is clear, whether the outcomes chain organizes the theory, whether outcomes address the problem, whether mechanisms are articulated, and whether the logical argument is plausible.
External validation asks whether the theory is supported by research, consistent with accepted theories, attentive to context, ethically defensible, and appropriate for its intended use. This turns program theory into an evaluand: something that can itself be assessed and improved.
The authors also emphasize stakeholder engagement and response. A critique is valuable only if its results inform revision, evaluation design, or decisions about whether the theory is good enough for the intended purpose.

title: 10. Critiquing Program Theory
text:
Key Concepts

Internal critique: assessment of coherence, clarity, and logical structure.
External validation: assessment against evidence, accepted theory, context, ethics, and use.
Mechanism articulation: explaining how activities or conditions produce change.
Fitness for purpose: whether the theory is worth its cost for the intended use.

This note summarises Chapter 8, “Developing a Theory of Action,” from Funnell and Rogers’ Purposeful Program Theory (Funnell and Rogers 2011). It is a paraphrased study note based on the extracted source text, not a substitute for the chapter.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Core Argument
A theory of action explains what the program will do to activate the theory of change, including resources, activities, outputs, success criteria, assumptions, and factors that affect achievement.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Study Summary
Where Chapter 7 concentrates on the desired change pathway, Chapter 8 asks how the program is expected to make that pathway happen. The theory of action identifies resources, activities, outputs, implementation features, assumptions, and external factors that influence whether intended outcomes are achieved.
The chapter gives substantial attention to success criteria and desired attributes. These convert broad outcome language into evaluative standards, making it clearer what would count as good performance. It also emphasizes realistic success criteria, especially when outcomes depend on factors outside program control.
The authors connect theory of action and theory of change through comparison: activities and outputs should be checked against the factors believed to influence outcomes. If the program does not act on the causal factors identified in the theory of change, the model exposes a strategic mismatch.

title: 8. Developing a Theory of Action
text:
Key Concepts

Theory of action: the account of what the program does and how delivery is expected to support change.
Success criteria: standards or desired attributes used to judge whether outcomes are good enough.
Program factors and nonprogram factors: influences inside and outside the intervention’s control.
Theory matrix: a way to align outcomes, assumptions, activities, and evidence needs.

Check whether activities are logically connected to the causal factors they are meant to influence.
Track external factors that can enable, constrain, or explain outcomes.

Part 3: Developing and Representing Program Theory Source PDF pages 122-175 Chapter 6

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Core Argument
Developing a program theory is a methodological choice, not just a workshop activity; evaluators must decide who participates, what evidence informs the theory, how conflict is handled, how much resource to invest, and when to revisit the theory.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Study Summary
This long chapter sets out process options for identifying or developing program theory. It contrasts staff-led, evaluator-facilitated, stakeholder-engaged, deductive, inductive, and mental-model approaches, arguing that the right mix depends on purpose, context, evidence availability, and stakeholder politics.
The authors give detailed attention to workshops and interviews, but they treat these as tools within a broader design. Workshops can surface divergent theories, build ownership, and negotiate shared models; they can also suppress disagreement if poorly facilitated. Interviews and document review may be better when perspectives are sensitive or dispersed.
The chapter is also realistic about challenges: conflicting program theories, little research evidence, reluctance to disclose assumptions, weak engagement, and limited resources. The response is not to avoid theory development but to make trade-offs explicit and revisit the theory as evidence and circumstances change.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Key Concepts

Mental models: implicit stakeholder beliefs about how the program works.
Deductive development: building from formal theory, prior research, or established causal knowledge.
Inductive development: building from observations, cases, data, and practice experience.
Relative program theory: a theory that is fit for a purpose and moment rather than final for all uses.

title: 6. Processes to Identify or Develop a Program Theory
text:
Practical Implications

Design the theory-development process as carefully as the representation.
Combine stakeholder knowledge, research evidence, and empirical observation where possible.
Document disagreements and uncertainty instead of forcing premature consensus.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Core Argument
A program theory should be scoped around intended users, uses, and timing; the same intervention can need different theories for planning, communication, management, monitoring, evaluation, or evidence-informed policy.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Study Summary
This chapter shifts attention from what a program theory contains to what it is for. A theory intended for early design and stakeholder alignment may need different detail from one used for performance monitoring, impact evaluation, or synthesis across programs.
The authors emphasize users as well as uses. Funders, managers, delivery staff, partners, participants, evaluators, and policymakers may need different representations and may also bring different knowledge about how the intervention is expected to work. Timing matters because a theory built before implementation supports design choices, while a theory reconstructed later supports evaluation and learning from what actually happened.
The chapter presents program theory as a flexible tool across the program cycle. Its usefulness depends on making the intended use explicit before deciding who participates, what evidence is needed, how detailed the model should be, and how formal the representation needs to become.

title: 4. Scoping Intended Uses
text:
Key Concepts

Intended users: people who will directly use the program theory or evaluation findings.
Intended uses: the decisions, actions, or learning processes the theory should support.
Timing: the point in the program cycle when the theory is developed or revised.
Use-sensitive design: tailoring theory development and representation to practical use.

Identify who must understand, own, challenge, or apply the theory.
Revise the theory when the program moves from design to implementation to evaluation.

Part 1: Key Ideas in Program Theory Source PDF pages 42-63 Chapter 2

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Core Argument
Program theory is not a new invention but a family of related ideas with varied terminology, intellectual roots, and practical traditions; evaluators need to understand the vocabulary well enough to choose terms that support use rather than confusion.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Study Summary
The chapter places program theory in a longer history of evaluation, planning, social science, and management practice. Rather than presenting one settled method, it shows how different traditions have emphasized causal explanation, planning logic, accountability, learning, stakeholder communication, and evidence synthesis.
A substantial part of the chapter is terminological. Terms such as program theory, theory of change, theory of action, logic model, logframe, results chain, outcomes chain, and evaluation model are used differently across organizations and countries. The authors treat terminology as consequential because vocabulary shapes what people attend to and what they overlook.
The chapter distinguishes key ideas without insisting on one universal label. The practical message is to define terms explicitly in each project, align language with users, and avoid assuming that a familiar diagram or phrase carries the same meaning for every stakeholder.

title: 2. Variations of Program Theory over Time
text:
Key Concepts

Theory of change: the causal account of how and why change is expected to happen.
Theory of action: the account of what the program will do to activate or support the theory of change.
Logic model: a representation of program components and expected links, often but not always diagrammatic.
Terminological fit: choosing words that make the theory usable for the intended audience.

Define the working vocabulary at the start of a program theory exercise.
Expect organizational templates to encode assumptions about what counts as a good theory.
Treat debates over terms as design signals, not merely semantic disputes.

text:
Core Argument
The section warns that logic models can become misleading when programs combine intervention delivery with wider system change.

title: When Logic Models Do Not Work as Expected
text:
Study Summary
The section warns that logic models can become misleading when programs combine intervention delivery with wider system change.
The problem is not that logic models are useless, but that they need to be applied with judgment about what the program is trying to change and how stable the causal path is.
When the model cannot represent system dynamics or multiple layers of action, evaluators should supplement it with richer program theory.

title: Evaluation Design and Its Components
text:
Study Summary
The section treats evaluation design as an explicit agreement about purpose, methodology, program description, and the information needs the evaluation must serve.
A useful design does more than select methods. It asks whether the program is described coherently enough to support evaluation and whether stakeholders need a logic model, a fuller program theory, or both.
The practical lesson is proportionality: choose enough structure to make the evaluation credible and useful without forcing every program into the same template.

title: Chapter 25: Metaevaluation: Evaluating Evaluations
text:
Core Argument
Metaevaluation is the professional requirement to evaluate evaluations for improvement, accountability, and public trust.

title: Chapter 25: Metaevaluation: Evaluating Evaluations
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 25 defines metaevaluation as evaluating evaluations. Metaevaluators can support evaluators formatively by reviewing plans, procedures, reports, and impacts, and summatively by judging the completed evaluation’s utility, feasibility, propriety, accuracy, and accountability.
The standards-and-metaevaluation seed note belongs here because this chapter turns standards into a direct quality-assurance process. Standards-based metaevaluation is treated as analogous to auditing in accounting: a profession should not ask society to trust its products without independent scrutiny.
The chapter presents tasks, arrangements, cases, checklists, and qualifications for metaevaluation. It distinguishes metaevaluation from meta-analysis: the former judges an evaluation’s quality, while the latter synthesizes results across studies.
The authors also add judgment about scale and context. Not every small local improvement study needs an independent metaevaluation, but high-stakes, public, controversial, or widely used evaluations require stronger review.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses metaevaluation, formative metaevaluation, summative metaevaluation, and standards-based review while moving through rationale and definition, standards and caveats, and responsibilities. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Five, this chapter asks how evaluation itself is judged, governed, and made durable. It connects the book’s standards language to organizational routines so that evaluation quality is not left to isolated projects or individual evaluator preference.

title: Chapter 23: Analyzing and Synthesizing Information
text:
Core Argument
Analysis breaks evidence into interpretable findings; synthesis combines those findings with values to reach justified conclusions.

title: Chapter 23: Analyzing and Synthesizing Information
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 23 moves from information collection to analysis, synthesis, and judgment. The evaluator must answer descriptive, relational, and causal questions, but also combine evidence into bottom-line conclusions about value.
Quantitative analysis includes descriptive statistics, relational analysis, causal analysis, statistical significance, practical significance, confidence intervals, and effect sizes. The chapter emphasizes choosing methods whose assumptions fit the data and evaluation questions.
Qualitative analysis is treated as systematic discovery and interpretation. Evaluators define document sets, mark records, read samples, create grounded categories, code materials, summarize findings, and track evidence so claims can be defended.
Synthesis is the distinctive evaluative step. It combines findings across methods and sources, brings facts together with values, and resolves multiple value perspectives enough to support justified conclusions about merit, worth, significance, and probity.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses analysis, synthesis, quantitative analysis, and qualitative analysis while moving through rationale for conclusion-oriented evaluation, quantitative analysis, and qualitative analysis. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Four, this chapter shifts attention from approach selection to execution. It is most useful when read as procedural guidance that still depends on earlier choices about purpose, standards, users, evaluand boundaries, and the kind of value judgment the evaluation must support.

title: Chapter 21: Contracting Evaluations
text:
Core Argument
Evaluation contracts and memoranda of agreement protect evaluation quality by making roles, rights, questions, evidence, reporting, and follow-up explicit in advance.

title: Chapter 21: Contracting Evaluations
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 21 ties contracting to design and budgeting. Once an evaluation design and budget are accepted, evaluator and client still need an enforceable or mutually binding agreement that governs how the evaluation will proceed.
The chapter covers contracts and memoranda of agreement. It stresses that agreements should identify the evaluand, purpose, client, audiences, evaluator authority, standards, questions, information requirements, methods, analysis, reporting, schedule, budget, ownership, and follow-up responsibilities.
Advance agreement is especially important for information access, stakeholder cooperation, report review, dissemination, and dealing with potentially harmful findings. These topics become much harder to negotiate after the evaluation is underway.
The contracting checklist is not reproduced in the notes, but its function is central: it turns expectations into explicit commitments so that evaluation independence, feasibility, propriety, and utility are less vulnerable to later conflict.

title: Chapter 19: Designing Evaluations
text:
Core Argument
Evaluation design is an integrated set of decisions about purpose, users, questions, values, evidence, analysis, reporting, administration, and use.

title: Chapter 19: Designing Evaluations
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 19 defines an evaluation design as the decision architecture required to carry out a study. It includes the evaluand, audiences, questions, values, criteria, information sources, methods, analysis, reporting, schedule, administration, and use support.
The chapter uses CIPP and standards as design resources, but the generic checklist is intended to work across approaches. The design must be detailed enough to guide action while remaining adaptable when formative or responsive evaluations surface new questions.
Information planning is treated as part of design, not a later methods step. Evaluators should identify sources, instruments, sampling procedures, multiple evidence points, data control, analysis assumptions, and synthesis plans before fieldwork begins.
The chapter also connects design to budgeting, contracting, reporting, and use. A design that ignores resources, roles, communication, or file management is incomplete even if its methods section looks technically sound.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses evaluation design, evaluation question, value criteria, and information source while moving through definition and design skills, CIPP-based example, and standards in design. title: Chapter 17: Identifying and Assessing Evaluation Opportunities
text:
Core Argument
Before accepting evaluation work, evaluators should systematically identify, screen, and improve opportunities so that the assignment can be conducted credibly.

title: Chapter 17: Identifying and Assessing Evaluation Opportunities
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 17 begins the procedural part of the book. It addresses how evaluators find opportunities such as RFPs, RFQs, internal assignments, sole-source requests, evaluator-initiated opportunities, grants, contracts, and cooperative agreements.
The chapter’s central practical question is whether an opportunity is worth pursuing. Evaluators should consider purpose, feasibility, ethical conditions, political climate, access to information, reporting freedom, resources, timeline, and fit with their competence.
Internal evaluators sometimes cannot decline assignments. The authors therefore discuss how to ameliorate weak conditions: negotiate scope, clarify standards, document limitations, protect independence where possible, and communicate risks to clients.
The chapter reframes opportunity assessment as quality assurance. The earliest decision about whether and how to engage an assignment can determine whether later design, evidence collection, and reporting will be credible.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses evaluation opportunity, RFP, RFQ, and sole-source request while moving through types of opportunities, sources of information, and questions for deciding whether to pursue. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Four, this chapter shifts attention from approach selection to execution. It is most useful when read as procedural guidance that still depends on earlier choices about purpose, standards, users, evaluand boundaries, and the kind of value judgment the evaluation must support.

title: Chapter 15: Robert Stake’s Responsive or Stakeholder-centered Evaluation Approach
text:
Core Argument
Stake’s responsive evaluation centers stakeholder concerns, program transactions, and plural value perspectives rather than fixed objectives alone.

title: Chapter 15: Robert Stake’s Responsive or Stakeholder-centered Evaluation Approach
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 15 traces Stake’s development from the countenance model to responsive or stakeholder-centered evaluation. The authors place Stake in the social agenda and advocacy tradition because his work emphasizes pluralism, interaction, local autonomy, and service to stakeholders.
The countenance model focuses attention on antecedents, transactions, and outcomes, and on the difference between description and judgment. Responsive evaluation extends this by letting stakeholder issues and observed program activity shape inquiry as the evaluation unfolds.
Stake’s approach rejects the idea that the evaluator can always impose one final authoritative value frame. It values subjective information, stakeholder judgments, and thick descriptions of what a program is doing and how people experience it.
The chapter also positions Stake in debate with Scriven. Where Scriven stresses independent consumer judgment, Stake stresses responsiveness to those involved in the program. The evaluator’s professional judgment still matters, but it is exercised through interaction and interpretation.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses responsive evaluation, stakeholder-centered evaluation, countenance model, and antecedents while moving through Stake’s evaluation philosophy, countenance article, and responsive evaluation. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Three, this chapter turns a named approach into a design option. The issue for study is not whether the model has a memorable label, but which evaluation tasks it handles well, what ethical stance it implies, and where it needs supplementation by standards, mixed methods, or metaevaluation.

title: Chapter 13: Daniel Stufflebeam’s CIPP Model for Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
The CIPP model links context, input, process, and product evaluation to support both improvement and accountability across a program’s life cycle.

title: Chapter 13: Daniel Stufflebeam’s CIPP Model for Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 13 gives the fullest account of Stufflebeam’s CIPP model. The model defines evaluation as delineating, obtaining, reporting, and applying descriptive and judgmental information about an object’s value, with attention to quality, worth, probity, equity, cost, efficiency, safety, and significance.
The four CIPP categories organize different decision needs. Context evaluation clarifies setting, needs, problems, assets, and goals. Input evaluation assesses strategies, plans, resources, and alternatives. Process evaluation monitors implementation and costs. Product evaluation identifies intended and unintended outcomes.
The current CIPP seed note belongs here because it captures the chapter’s overview: CIPP is designed for formative and summative use, works across sectors, and asks evaluators to attend to stakeholders, values, standards, procedures, and systems.
The standards-and-metaevaluation seed note also partly belongs here. The CIPP chapter grounds the model in professional standards, an objectivist orientation, democratic values, improvement, stakeholder service, and internal or external metaevaluation as part of responsible practice.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses CIPP, context evaluation, input evaluation, and process evaluation while moving through overview and roots, applications, and definitions of evaluation. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Three, this chapter turns a named approach into a design option. The issue for study is not whether the model has a memorable label, but which evaluation tasks it handles well, what ethical stance it implies, and where it needs supplementation by standards, mixed methods, or metaevaluation.

title: Chapter 11: Experimental and Quasi-experimental Design Evaluations
text:
Core Argument
Experimental and quasi-experimental designs are valuable for causal inference when conditions fit, but they are not sufficient by themselves for many evaluation purposes.

title: Chapter 11: Experimental and Quasi-experimental Design Evaluations
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 11 explains the logic of experimental and quasi-experimental design evaluations. Random assignment is the key feature of the strongest experimental designs, while quasi-experimental designs use other assignment, comparison, and measurement strategies when randomization is not feasible.
The authors present these designs as part of the evaluator’s repertoire, not as the universal model of evaluation. They are especially useful for estimating effects, but they can be weak on context, implementation, side effects, values, and ongoing improvement feedback.
The chapter connects older scientific evaluation traditions to contemporary field trials and quasi-experimental design notation. Design elements include assignment, measurement timing, comparison groups, treatments, and validity threats.
A major practical warning is that causal inference requirements can conflict with real program conditions. Evaluators must consider feasibility, ethics, stakeholder acceptance, treatment stability, and whether a narrow effect estimate will answer the evaluation’s value questions.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses random assignment, quasi-experimental design, comparison group, and validity threat while moving through chapter overview, scientific approach, and randomized field trials. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Three, this chapter turns a named approach into a design option. The issue for study is not whether the model has a memorable label, but which evaluation tasks it handles well, what ethical stance it implies, and where it needs supplementation by standards, mixed methods, or metaevaluation.

title: Chapter 09: Eclectic Evaluation Approaches
text:
Core Argument
Eclectic approaches deliberately borrow from multiple evaluation traditions to serve users and contexts, but they must still meet standards for sound evaluation.

title: Chapter 09: Eclectic Evaluation Approaches
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 9 focuses on utilization-focused evaluation and participatory evaluation as eclectic approaches. They do not fit cleanly into quasi-evaluation, improvement-accountability, or social agenda categories because they select concepts and methods according to user needs.
Utilization-focused evaluation is organized around intended users and intended uses. The evaluator works with primary users to focus questions, select methods, interpret evidence, and support use. The chapter treats this as powerful but dependent on user commitment and evaluator skill.
Participatory evaluation also involves stakeholders, but it is less restricted to a predefined group of intended users. It can build ownership and capacity, while raising questions about independence, representativeness, and control over judgments.
The authors’ standard-based frame remains in place. Eclecticism is not permission to improvise without discipline. It is defensible only when the selected combination of methods, roles, and values can be justified against utility, feasibility, propriety, accuracy, and accountability.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses eclectic evaluation, utilization-focused evaluation, intended users, and intended use while moving through definition of eclectic approaches, utilization-focused evaluation, and participatory evaluation. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Two, this chapter should be read as part of Stufflebeam and Coryn’s standards-based assessment of evaluation approaches. Its classifications and ratings are their judgments about how well each approach supports defensible evaluation, not a neutral consensus ranking of the whole field.

title: Chapter 07: Improvement- and Accountability-oriented Evaluation Approaches
text:
Core Argument
Improvement- and accountability-oriented approaches are closest to the authors’ definition of evaluation because they explicitly assess value while serving decisions and public responsibility.

title: Chapter 07: Improvement- and Accountability-oriented Evaluation Approaches
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 7 describes approaches that seek to determine an evaluand’s value rather than only answer a technical subquestion. The chapter covers decision- and accountability-oriented studies, consumer-oriented studies, and accreditation or certification.
Decision- and accountability-oriented studies are represented mainly through CIPP. They combine improvement feedback with retrospective judgment, attend to stakeholders, and use multiple methods to assess context, plans, implementation, outcomes, and value.
Consumer-oriented studies, associated with Scriven, are more directly focused on consumers’ needs, alternatives, costs, and comparative value. Accreditation and certification add a standards-based institutional form in which a program or organization is judged against external criteria.
The authors present these approaches as generally stronger than quasi-evaluations because they confront value. Their differences matter: CIPP is more improvement and systems oriented, consumer-oriented evaluation is more independent and comparative, and accreditation is more rule-bound.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses improvement, accountability, decision-oriented evaluation, and CIPP while moving through definition and functions, general strengths and weaknesses, and decision- and accountability-oriented studies. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Two, this chapter should be read as part of Stufflebeam and Coryn’s standards-based assessment of evaluation approaches. Its classifications and ratings are their judgments about how well each approach supports defensible evaluation, not a neutral consensus ranking of the whole field.

title: Chapter 05: Pseudoevaluations
text:
Core Argument
Pseudoevaluations use the appearance of evaluation while failing to provide independent, credible, and value-focused assessment.

title: Chapter 05: Pseudoevaluations
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 5 identifies approaches that the authors judge to be invalid or dangerously incomplete when presented as evaluation. The point is not that every activity described is always useless, but that each can mislead when it substitutes for defensible assessment of merit and worth.
The authors discuss politically controlled studies, public relations studies, accountability theatre, and other forms in which the evaluation process is bent toward a predetermined interest. The harm is practical and ethical: weak evidence can legitimate bad decisions and reduce confidence in evaluation.
A recurring warning is that good intentions do not rescue a flawed evaluation structure. Even approaches meant to empower stakeholders can become pseudoevaluative if they give interested parties control over evidence quality and judgment while presenting the result as independent evaluation.
The chapter is a professional ethics checkpoint. Evaluators need to recognize assignments that would compromise independence, distort reporting, hide findings, or confuse satisfaction feedback with a credible value judgment.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses pseudoevaluation, political control, public relations evaluation, and customer feedback while moving through definition of pseudoevaluation, types of misleading studies, and political and public relations risks. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Two, this chapter should be read as part of Stufflebeam and Coryn’s standards-based assessment of evaluation approaches. Its classifications and ratings are their judgments about how well each approach supports defensible evaluation, not a neutral consensus ranking of the whole field.

title: Chapter 03: Standards for Program Evaluations
text:
Core Argument
Professional standards are the field’s practical infrastructure for making evaluations useful, feasible, proper, accurate, and accountable.

title: Chapter 03: Standards for Program Evaluations
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 3 treats standards as protection against poor, biased, or incredible evaluation work. Standards help evaluators and clients agree on what an evaluation should meet, provide common language, and create grounds for judging plans, conduct, reports, and organizations.
The chapter compares three major sources of guidance: the Joint Committee’s Program Evaluation Standards, the American Evaluation Association’s Guiding Principles for Evaluators, and Government Auditing Standards. Each has a different origin and emphasis, but all can inform program evaluation practice.
The Joint Committee standards receive special attention because their categories of utility, feasibility, propriety, accuracy, and evaluation accountability recur throughout the book. The authors use those categories later to classify and rate approaches and to structure metaevaluation.
The practical message is not that evaluators should mechanically cite standards. They should select relevant standards, orient stakeholders to them, apply them throughout the evaluation, and decide whether independent metaevaluation is warranted.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses utility, feasibility, propriety, and accuracy while moving through need for standards, background of standards, and Joint Committee standards. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the foundations that make later model ratings and procedural guidance intelligible. It supplies vocabulary for judging approaches, reading standards, and distinguishing evaluation from narrower forms of measurement, monitoring, research, or administrative review.

title: Chapter 01: Overview of the Evaluation Field
text:
Core Argument
Evaluation is presented as a systematic value-oriented discipline that society needs for improvement, accountability, and protection from weak or unsafe services.

title: Chapter 01: Overview of the Evaluation Field
text:
Study Summary
The opening chapter separates formal evaluation from everyday judgment. Informal evaluation is unavoidable, but it is often unsystematic and weakly documented. Formal evaluation is treated as a disciplined process for judging merit, worth, probity, safety, effectiveness, efficiency, and related criteria.
The chapter broadens evaluation beyond objectives and outcomes. Programs, policies, services, products, personnel systems, and organizations can all be evaluands. A sound evaluation asks what value criteria matter, whose needs are at stake, what evidence is credible, and how findings should be communicated.
Formative and summative uses organize much of the chapter. Formative evaluation supports development and improvement, while summative evaluation supports overall judgment, accountability, adoption, or discontinuation. The authors stress that the two uses can interact and should not be treated as mutually exclusive.
The historical review positions evaluation as a profession that grew through educational measurement, objectives-based evaluation, federal program accountability, professional standards, and later multidisciplinary expansion. The field’s history is used as a warning against narrow method loyalties and as a rationale for standards-based practice.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses formal evaluation, informal evaluation, evaluand, and merit and worth while moving through definitions and objects of evaluation, criteria for judging value, and formative and summative uses. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the foundations that make later model ratings and procedural guidance intelligible. It supplies vocabulary for judging approaches, reading standards, and distinguishing evaluation from narrower forms of measurement, monitoring, research, or administrative review.

Evaluation Practice Implications

Identify real users, not abstract audiences.
Focus questions around intended uses before choosing methods.
Engage users in methods trade-offs so credibility and utility are negotiated together.
Plan interpretation and action, not only reporting.

title: Chapter 02: Evaluation Theory
text:
Core Argument
Evaluation approaches should be developed and tested as theories rather than treated only as branded models or practitioner traditions.

title: Chapter 02: Evaluation Theory
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 2 asks what it would mean for evaluation to have stronger theory. The authors distinguish a theory from an approach or model: a theory should contain conceptual, hypothetical, pragmatic, and ethical principles that explain and guide evaluation practice.
The chapter argues that the evaluation field has generated many useful concepts, standards, approaches, and practical guidelines, but fewer well-tested propositions about what evaluation actions produce useful consequences in particular contexts. This makes humility important when applying any model.
Criteria for judging evaluation theories include their contribution to professionalizing evaluation, supporting research, guiding planning, structuring implementation, and strengthening use. The existing major-section note on theory development belongs here because it captures the authors’ argument that standards and metaevaluation should help convert promising approaches into better tested theories.
The chapter frames theory development as both creative and empirical. Evaluators need conceptual imagination, but they also need metaevaluations and research on evaluation to test whether an approach actually improves utility, feasibility, propriety, accuracy, accountability, and use.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses evaluation theory, evaluation model, evaluation approach, and theory development while moving through role of theory in the field, theory versus model or approach, and components of sound theory. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to the foundations that make later model ratings and procedural guidance intelligible. It supplies vocabulary for judging approaches, reading standards, and distinguishing evaluation from narrower forms of measurement, monitoring, research, or administrative review.

title: Chapter 04: Background for Assessing Evaluation Approaches
text:
Core Argument
Evaluation approaches can be compared by how fully they support defensible judgments of value and by how well they meet professional standards.

title: Chapter 04: Background for Assessing Evaluation Approaches
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 4 sets up the taxonomy used in Part Two. The authors classify twenty-three approaches into pseudoevaluations, quasi-evaluations, improvement- and accountability-oriented approaches, social agenda and advocacy approaches, and eclectic approaches.
The chapter’s main function is methodological. It explains the categories, reviews earlier classification efforts, and defines a set of descriptors used to characterize each approach. These descriptors cover purpose, questions, methods, pioneers, use conditions, strengths, and weaknesses.
The taxonomy is explicitly evaluative. Approaches are not merely listed; they are judged according to whether they support credible assessment of merit and worth. Pseudoevaluations are rejected because they mislead, while legitimate approaches are still assessed for scope and standard compliance.
The chapter prepares readers to avoid method-first thinking. An approach may be useful for a narrow purpose and still be inadequate as a full evaluation. This distinction matters throughout Chapters 5 through 10.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses approach classification, pseudoevaluation, quasi-evaluation, and improvement and accountability while moving through rationale for classification, previous classifications, and five categories. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Two, this chapter should be read as part of Stufflebeam and Coryn’s standards-based assessment of evaluation approaches. Its classifications and ratings are their judgments about how well each approach supports defensible evaluation, not a neutral consensus ranking of the whole field.

title: Chapter 06: Quasi-evaluation Studies
text:
Core Argument
Quasi-evaluations can answer focused questions well, but their narrow scope means they should not be mistaken for full judgments of merit and worth.

title: Chapter 06: Quasi-evaluation Studies
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 6 covers approaches that are technically useful but limited by question scope, method scope, or both. Objectives-based studies, success case work, value-added assessment, experiments, cost studies, connoisseurship, theory-based evaluation, and meta-analysis can all serve evaluators.
The authors’ caution is that a high-quality answer to a narrow question is not necessarily a complete evaluation. For example, objective attainment does not by itself show whether objectives were worth pursuing, whether needs were met, or whether side effects mattered.
The chapter is not anti-method. It asks evaluators to know these approaches well enough to use them appropriately. Narrow studies may be valuable when clients need focused, timely evidence, but the reporting should make the limits explicit.
The chapter also prepares readers for later chapters on experimental designs and case studies. Some approaches receive fuller treatment in Part Three because they are common and powerful, but their limitations still need to be understood.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses quasi-evaluation, objectives-based study, success case method, and value-added assessment while moving through definition and functions, strengths and weaknesses, and eight quasi-evaluation approaches. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Two, this chapter should be read as part of Stufflebeam and Coryn’s standards-based assessment of evaluation approaches. Its classifications and ratings are their judgments about how well each approach supports defensible evaluation, not a neutral consensus ranking of the whole field.

title: Chapter 08: Social Agenda and Advocacy Evaluation Approaches
text:
Core Argument
Social agenda and advocacy approaches bring democratic and justice commitments into evaluation, but they require strong safeguards against bias, impracticality, and loss of closure.

title: Chapter 08: Social Agenda and Advocacy Evaluation Approaches
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 8 examines approaches that use evaluation to serve equity, democratic participation, and social change. Responsive or stakeholder-centered evaluation, constructivist evaluation, deliberative democratic evaluation, and transformative evaluation all give stakeholders a central role.
The authors recognize the strengths of these approaches: they engage affected groups, surface multiple value perspectives, and challenge evaluations that ignore power and injustice. These strengths make them important correctives to technocratic evaluation.
The chapter also raises concerns. Extensive participation can be expensive and difficult to close; relativist assumptions can make bottom-line judgment harder; advocacy commitments can create bias risks if not checked by evidence, standards, and metaevaluation.
The practical lesson is to separate legitimate value commitments from uncontrolled advocacy. A justice-oriented evaluation still needs explicit standards, defensible evidence, transparent roles, and credible reporting.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses responsive evaluation, constructivist evaluation, deliberative democratic evaluation, and transformative evaluation while moving through overview of advocacy approaches, responsive or stakeholder-centered evaluation, and constructivist evaluation. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Two, this chapter should be read as part of Stufflebeam and Coryn’s standards-based assessment of evaluation approaches. Its classifications and ratings are their judgments about how well each approach supports defensible evaluation, not a neutral consensus ranking of the whole field.

title: Chapter 10: Best Approaches for Twenty-first-century Evaluations
text:
Core Argument
Stufflebeam and Coryn use the program evaluation standards to compare selected approaches and argue that model choice should be standards-based rather than fashion-based.

title: Chapter 10: Best Approaches for Twenty-first-century Evaluations
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 10 functions like a consumer report on selected evaluation approaches. The authors rate nine approaches against utility, feasibility, propriety, accuracy, evaluation accountability, and overall merit using a standards-derived checklist.
The chapter is explicit about selection and judgment. The chosen approaches represent several categories, but they are not the only legitimate approaches. The ratings reflect the authors’ methodology, experience, and criteria, so they should be read as a structured expert judgment rather than a universal ranking.
CIPP, utilization-focused, constructivist, responsive or stakeholder-centered, and consumer-oriented approaches are among those the authors rate relatively well overall. Experimental, objectives-based, success case, and case study approaches receive more limited ratings because they often answer narrower questions or have lower utility/accountability ratings.
The practical value of the chapter is its method, not only its rankings. Evaluators can adapt the standards-based rating logic to assess approaches for a particular assignment, making assumptions and tradeoffs visible before committing to a design.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses standards-based comparison, approach rating, utility, and feasibility while moving through selection of approaches, rating methodology, and comparison of approaches. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Two, this chapter should be read as part of Stufflebeam and Coryn’s standards-based assessment of evaluation approaches. Its classifications and ratings are their judgments about how well each approach supports defensible evaluation, not a neutral consensus ranking of the whole field.

title: Chapter 12: Case Study Evaluations
text:
Core Argument
Case study evaluation provides rich, naturalistic understanding of a case, especially when evaluators need depth, context, and triangulated perspectives.

title: Chapter 12: Case Study Evaluations
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 12 treats case study evaluation as an in-depth, noninterventionist examination of a program, component, situation, or case in its natural setting. It is especially useful when the evaluator needs a holistic account rather than a controlled test.
The chapter contrasts Stake’s and Yin’s contributions. Stake’s approach emphasizes naturalistic, responsive understanding and the case’s complexity. Yin’s approach is more design-oriented and concerned with systematic case study logic for answering evaluation questions.
Information collection is central. The authors discuss documents, records, observation, interviews, site visits, content analysis, and triangulation. The evaluator follows trails of interest but still needs enough discipline to support credible interpretation.
The chapter also notes limits. Case studies can illuminate value and context, but they may not provide strong generalizability or confident recommendations unless designed carefully and connected to the evaluation’s purpose.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses case study, naturalistic inquiry, noninterventionist evaluation, and Stake while moving through overview of the approach, Stake’s case study evaluation, and Yin’s case study methodology. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Three, this chapter turns a named approach into a design option. The issue for study is not whether the model has a memorable label, but which evaluation tasks it handles well, what ethical stance it implies, and where it needs supplementation by standards, mixed methods, or metaevaluation.

title: Chapter 14: Michael Scriven’s Consumer-oriented Approach to Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
Scriven’s consumer-oriented approach asks evaluators to act as independent value judges on behalf of consumers and public welfare.

title: Chapter 14: Michael Scriven’s Consumer-oriented Approach to Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 14 reviews Scriven’s extensive contributions to evaluation. The authors emphasize his insistence that evaluation is fundamentally about judging value, not merely documenting objective attainment or producing value-free social science.
Consumer orientation means evaluating programs, services, or products in relation to consumer needs, alternatives, costs, and wider social significance. The evaluator is expected to function as an informed surrogate consumer with strong ethical responsibility.
The chapter covers formative and summative evaluation, goal-free evaluation, scoring, ranking, grading, apportioning, causal reasoning, and the Key Evaluation Checklist. These tools support Scriven’s broader effort to make evaluation a disciplined transdiscipline.
Scriven’s approach contrasts sharply with responsive or utilization-focused traditions. It places more weight on independent judgment and defensible criteria, while still recognizing that formative evaluation can improve what will later be judged summatively.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses consumer-oriented evaluation, surrogate consumer, formative evaluation, and summative evaluation while moving through Scriven’s orientation, critique of objectives-based evaluation, and formative and summative roles. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.

title: Chapter 16: Michael Patton’s Utilization-focused Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
Utilization-focused evaluation designs every major evaluation choice around intended users and intended uses.

title: Chapter 16: Michael Patton’s Utilization-focused Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 16 expands the utilization-focused approach introduced in Chapter 9. Patton’s central claim is that evaluation should be judged by its actual usefulness to specific primary intended users for specific intended uses.
The evaluator is not a detached judge in this approach. The evaluator facilitates decisions about focus, questions, values, methods, interpretation, and reporting with users who are expected to act on findings. This requires skill in group process, negotiation, values analysis, and standards.
The chapter contrasts utilization-focused evaluation with responsive and constructivist approaches. It shares their attention to stakeholders, but it is more explicit about selecting primary users and building the evaluation around expected use.
Limitations follow from the same strength. If intended users are poorly selected, uncommitted, unrepresentative, or unwilling to engage evidence, the evaluation can lose credibility or relevance. The approach also needs safeguards so use does not override propriety or accuracy.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses utilization-focused evaluation, primary intended users, intended use, and facilitation while moving through general aspects of UFE, values and judgments, and planning UFE. title: Chapter 18: First Steps in Addressing Evaluation Opportunities
text:
Core Argument
Once an opportunity is worth pursuing, evaluators need disciplined start-up work before writing or executing the evaluation design.

title: Chapter 18: First Steps in Addressing Evaluation Opportunities
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 18 covers the first operational steps after deciding to pursue an evaluation. These include defining team roles, recruiting staff and collaborators, assigning credit, clarifying the evaluation need, and obtaining institutional support.
The chapter emphasizes timing. Strong proposals and evaluations require early recruitment, early drafting, early review, and enough time to satisfy institutional and human-subjects requirements. These tasks are not administrative afterthoughts.
Stakeholder review panels receive specific attention. A well-composed panel can help clarify questions, improve access, review plans and draft reports, and increase relevance and credibility. A poorly composed panel can narrow perspective or create politics.
The practical message is that start-up work is part of evaluation quality. The design will be weaker if the team, institutional base, standards, permissions, and stakeholder relationships are improvised late.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses evaluation team, subcontracting, institutional support, and human subjects review while moving through team development, recruitment, and institutional support. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Four, this chapter shifts attention from approach selection to execution. It is most useful when read as procedural guidance that still depends on earlier choices about purpose, standards, users, evaluand boundaries, and the kind of value judgment the evaluation must support.

title: Chapter 20: Budgeting Evaluations
text:
Core Argument
Evaluation budgets are ethical and design instruments: they translate the promised scope of work into feasible, transparent, and defensible resource commitments.

title: Chapter 20: Budgeting Evaluations
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 20 explains why budgeting is inseparable from evaluation design. A budget should estimate the resources needed to deliver the agreed design and should make assumptions about tasks, personnel, travel, support, timing, indirect costs, and sponsor contributions visible.
The chapter distinguishes fixed and responsive conditions. Preordinate evaluations may support detailed up-front budgets, while responsive evaluations often need general budgets and periodic updating as user needs and interim findings develop.
Ethical budgeting matters. Underbudgeting can make a promised design impossible; padding or hiding costs can undermine trust; vague agreements can produce conflict. The authors treat budgeting as part of professional integrity.
The generic budgeting checklist is meant for constructing and reviewing budgets. It asks evaluators to link cost categories to design tasks, identify the needed level of detail, determine cost factors, and revisit the budget as plans become clearer.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses evaluation budget, line-item budget, modular budget, and cost factor while moving through budget rationale, ethical principles, and illustrative budgets. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.

title: Chapter 22: Collecting Evaluative Information
text:
Core Argument
Evaluative conclusions are only as defensible as the scope, quality, reliability, validity, and management of the information collected.

title: Chapter 22: Collecting Evaluative Information
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 22 gives practical advice on information collection. It begins from standards because the credibility of answers depends on whether information is relevant, sufficient, reliable, valid, and responsibly managed.
The chapter proposes a broad information framework that can cover background, context, structure, operations, costs, outputs, outcomes, side effects, and stakeholder perspectives. The goal is to avoid collecting only what is easy or familiar.
Reliability and validity are treated as practical obligations. Evaluators should train collectors, document procedures, use multiple sources and methods when needed, check data quality, protect records, and report threats to validity.
The chapter also recognizes feasibility. Adequate scope must be balanced against burden, time, and cost. The evaluator’s job is to gather enough credible information to answer priority questions and support justified conclusions.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses evaluative information, information scope, reliability, and validity while moving through standards for collection, information collection framework, and sampling. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.

title: Chapter 24: Communicating Evaluation Findings
text:
Core Argument
Evaluation findings have value only when communicated to intended users in credible, timely, accessible, and follow-up-supported ways.

title: Chapter 24: Communicating Evaluation Findings
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 24 argues that sound design, data collection, and analysis can still fail if findings are not communicated effectively. The evaluator must understand intended users, their information rights, their intended uses, and the formats that will support action.
Communication begins before the final report. Interim feedback can help improve programs, test interpretations, surface errors, and sustain use. The chapter links this especially to approaches such as CIPP, responsive evaluation, and utilization-focused evaluation.
Final reporting should synthesize findings around evaluation questions and value judgments rather than merely list methods or data sources. Reports may need different layers, formats, technical appendices, oral presentations, workshops, or other media.
Follow-up support is part of evaluation impact. Evaluators may need to help users interpret findings, plan responses, communicate with stakeholders, and document actions taken while preserving the boundaries of their role.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses intended users, interim feedback, final report, and dissemination while moving through reporting challenges, conditions for use, and interim feedback. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.

title: Chapter 26: Institutionalizing and Mainstreaming Evaluation
text:
Core Argument
Organizations should move evaluation from occasional projects to a mainstreamed system for planning, accountability, learning, and improvement.

title: Chapter 26: Institutionalizing and Mainstreaming Evaluation
text:
Study Summary
Chapter 26 closes the book by shifting from individual evaluation studies to organizational evaluation systems. The authors argue that organizations should institutionalize and mainstream evaluation so that it becomes part of routine governance and improvement.
Institutionalizing evaluation means establishing a durable system, roles, standards, policies, resources, and review mechanisms. Mainstreaming evaluation means embedding use of that system across levels and functions rather than leaving evaluation in a specialist corner.
The chapter reviews the book’s themes and translates them into organizational principles. Evaluation systems should be grounded in theory, standards, stakeholder engagement, sound methods, metaevaluation, communication, and systematic use.
A central practical tool is a checklist for establishing or strengthening an organizational evaluation system. The notes do not reproduce the checklist, but they treat it as a staged planning aid for design teams, review teams, standards selection, communication, capacity building, and periodic review.
For close reading, track how the chapter uses institutionalizing evaluation, mainstreaming evaluation, evaluation system, and design team while moving through review of book themes, definitions and rationale, and early efforts and advances. The notes below treat those moves as study scaffolding: they identify what the chapter asks the evaluator to notice, what judgment problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.
Within Part Five, this chapter asks how evaluation itself is judged, governed, and made durable. It connects the book’s standards language to organizational routines so that evaluation quality is not left to isolated projects or individual evaluator preference.

title: Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications Practice Implications
text:
Evaluation Practice Implications

Start every evaluation by clarifying the evaluand, users, purposes, value criteria, standards, and intended uses.
Choose models by fit to the assignment and standards, not by brand familiarity or method preference.
Distinguish focused studies from full evaluations of merit and worth, especially when using quasi-evaluation approaches.
Build standards into design, budgeting, contracting, information collection, reporting, and metaevaluation.
Use multiple sources and methods when a single procedure cannot support the required judgment.
Treat analysis and synthesis as value work: evidence must be connected to criteria before conclusions are justified.
Plan communication and follow-up early so findings reach intended users in forms they can use.
Use metaevaluation proportionate to stakes, risk, and public reliance on the evaluation.
For organizations, move from ad hoc studies toward mainstreamed evaluation systems with periodic review.

title: Evaluation Theory, Models, and Applications Practice Implications
text:
Applying the Notes
Use these implications as checks during scoping, proposal decisions, design review, method selection, reporting, follow-up, and metaevaluation. They should be adapted to the evaluation’s context, available resources, stakeholder needs, and standards obligations.

Quality assurance and early planning help maintain credibility when evaluation requirements change.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Core Argument
Program theory turns evaluation from a simple before-and-after judgement into an explanation of how an intervention is expected to generate change, where that explanation might fail, and what evidence would distinguish success, partial success, implementation failure, and theory failure.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Study Summary
The opening chapter uses a deliberately simple health example to show why an evaluation without program theory can identify an outcome pattern but cannot say much about why it occurred. A black-box evaluation can report whether results changed, but it gives weak guidance about which mechanisms worked, which activities mattered, or what should be changed next.
The chapter then reframes evaluation around a model of how resources, activities, outputs, intermediate outcomes, final outcomes, assumptions, and contextual factors are connected. This makes the evaluation more diagnostic: results can be interpreted against the expected chain of contribution rather than treated as a single program-versus-outcome comparison.
A central practical point is that failure, partial success, and apparent success all need explanation. Program theory helps evaluators identify whether the intervention was implemented as intended, whether the causal logic was plausible, whether context disrupted the pathway, and whether observed results can be credibly attributed to the program.

title: 1. The Essence of Program Theory
text:
Key Concepts

Black-box evaluation: an evaluation that relates an intervention to outcomes without unpacking the intervening causal pathway.
Program theory: an explicit account of how an intervention is expected to contribute to intended outcomes.
Implementation failure versus theory failure: a poor result may reflect weak delivery, weak causal assumptions, or both.
Partial success: evidence may support some links in the chain while exposing weak or missing links elsewhere.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Core Argument
The value of program theory depends on avoiding common myths and practical traps, especially the belief that drawing a diagram is enough or that one standard model can fit every evaluation purpose.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Study Summary
The chapter clears away myths that weaken practice: program theory is not brand new, there is not one correct way to do it, it can be a serious evaluation model, and it does not necessarily require excessive time. The authors also challenge the idea that a theory is untestable or that causality can simply be assumed once a model is drawn.
The traps are more operational. Program theory can become a compliance artifact, a vague outcomes list, an overcomplicated wall chart, or a model that ignores unintended outcomes and contextual variation. A particularly important trap is developing a theory for an evaluation and then failing to use it in evaluation design, data collection, analysis, and reporting.
The chapter’s stance is pragmatic: program theory should be purposeful, proportionate, explicit, and used. A modest but well-targeted theory is better than an elaborate representation that nobody uses to make decisions.

title: 3. Common Myths and Traps
text:
Key Concepts

Myth: a mistaken belief about what program theory is or requires.
Trap: a recurring practice problem that undermines the usefulness of a program theory.
Purposeful proportionality: investing enough effort for the intended use without turning the exercise into an end in itself.
Unintended results: outcomes that are outside the intended chain but still matter for evaluation.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Core Argument
The nature of the intervention and its setting should shape the program theory; simple, complicated, and complex situations require different assumptions about focus, governance, consistency, necessity, sufficiency, and change trajectories.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Study Summary
The chapter introduces distinctions among simple, complicated, and complex interventions. Simple situations have relatively direct and stable pathways. Complicated situations involve multiple components, actors, sites, or technical dependencies. Complex situations involve adaptation, emergence, feedback, and context-sensitive pathways.
The authors use these distinctions to warn against forcing every intervention into a linear model. A program theory may need to show multiple strands, variable implementation, different causal mechanisms in different settings, or pathways that emerge through learning. Governance and consistency also matter because centrally controlled programs differ from networked or locally adapted interventions.
The chapter’s practical value is diagnostic. Before choosing a representation or evaluation design, evaluators should ask what kind of intervention they are dealing with and what type of causal claim is reasonable.

title: 5. The Nature of the Situation and the Intervention
text:
Key Concepts

Simple, complicated, complex: a continuum for thinking about intervention structure and causal predictability.
Necessariness and sufficiency: whether a component is required for success and whether it can generate success on its own.
Change trajectory: the expected pattern and timing of change.
Governance and consistency: how control, variation, and adaptation affect the theory.
